summary: Fowler highlights an emerging trend of Extreme Programming projects achieving remarkably low defect rates—less than one production bug per month. He cites several examples: a food sorting machine company that dropped from 100 open bugs to one every couple of months, a Chrysler portal team that recorded exactly one bug in all of 2002 while releasing biweekly, and various Thoughtworks projects showing similar results. While emphasizing this is only achieved by a disciplined minority of XP teams, he concludes these rates prove such quality is achievable in software development.
key_points: XP projects are demonstrating defect rates below one production bug per month, a remarkable achievement
Teams achieving these rates tend to be highly disciplined with experienced XP leadership
Low defect rates don't require sacrificing delivery speed—one team released every week or two while maintaining quality
This is not universal to XP adoption, but serious XP adoption commonly yields significant defect reduction
Other processes might achieve similar results, but XP is proving to be an effective tool for getting there
money_quotes: One small but growing trend that particularly interests me is the small but growing number of XP projects that have very low defect rates, by which I mean less than one production bug per month.
Although they started off at the shocking defect rate of one a month, during 2002 they recorded exactly one bug against their system.
During this time they were releasing new version of their software every week or two.
One of these found pre-install government certification reduced from weeks to days.
It does seem to be a common result that teams that do a serious adoption of XP see significantly lower bug rates.
The teams seem to be pretty disciplined and led by people who have a year or two of XP under their belt.
But what I can conclude is that these kinds of bug rates are achievable in software development.
key_insight: Disciplined XP teams are proving that near-zero defect rates are achievable in production software, challenging assumptions about inevitable bugginess in software development.
